flashcards.back: A constraint where one set of attributes uniquely determines another set. Denoted as X → Y, meaning X values determine Y values.
Three inference rules: 1) Reflexivity 2) Augmentation 3) Transitivity. Basis for deriving all functional dependencies.
An FD X → Y where Y is a subset of X. Always holds true (e.g., AB → A).
Set of all attributes functionally determined by a given attribute set X, denoted as X⁺.
FDs identify redundant data. Normal forms (3NF/BCNF) use FDs to eliminate update anomalies.
BCNF requires all determinants to be superkeys. 3NF allows transitive dependencies if dependent is prime.
Simplified set of FDs with no redundant dependencies and minimal left-hand sides.
An FD X → Y where no proper subset of X determines Y (e.g., {A,B} → C, but neither A nor B alone determines C).
Minimal superkey that uniquely identifies tuples. All non-prime attributes must fully depend on it.
Decomposing a relation into smaller relations without losing information. Natural join recreates original.
An attribute that is part of any candidate key.
For every non-trivial FD X → Y, X must be a superkey.
